data.title: Gold's Basel III Boost: Why KBWB Could Shine In 2025
data.description:
Gold shines in the eyes of many market participants. The precious metal soared to $3,500 per Troy ounce earlier this year, and while it has pulled back, traders, investors, and now regulators are taking a shine to this safe-haven asset.

A recent Gallup poll revealed that Americans view gold as a better long-term investment than stocks, second only to real estate (which is rarely not the top spot in that survey). Moreover, central banks have been major net gold buyers in the last decade-plus, which provides a fundamental, often price-insensitive demand source.

Amid what’s clearly a bull market in sentiment and price action, global lawmakers are now entering the scene.

Effective July 1, Basel III “Endgame” rules will reportedly reclassify gold as a Tier 1 High-Quality Liquid Asset, equivalent to cash or Treasurys. That’s significant news because it means US banks will be able to consider gold holdings at 100% of market value toward core capital reserves. Previously, gold was a Tier 3 asset, which meant it had to be discounted by 50% on bank balance sheets.

The upgrade comes at a key time for large and mid-sized US financial institutions—the Q1 earnings season featured increases in loan-loss provisions. With gold now counting more toward Tier 1 capital, bank assets could now be used for more pro-growth initiatives, such as capital markets activities or doling out loans to would-be borrowers.

Amid these changes, I remain bullish on the Invesco KBW Bank ETF (NASDAQ:KBWB). Shares are flat since my December 2024 analysis, outperforming the S&P 500 by about three percentage points. I will note that the fund remains a compelling value, and there are firm signs of a technical bottom.

Gold Catching Investors' Eyes

Heavy Central Bank Gold Buying

According to the issuer, KBWB is based on the KBW Nasdaq Bank Index. The ETF will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in the securities that comprise the underlying index. Using a modified-market cap-weighted index of companies primarily engaged in US banking activities, the fund is composed of large national US money centers, regional banks, and thrift institutions that are publicly traded in the US. KBWB is rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly.

Despite flat returns so far in 2025, KBWB’s assets under management has swelled from $2.8 billion last December to $3.8 billion today. Its annual expenses ratio is low to moderate at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about a full percentage point above that of the S&P 500 at 2.39% as of May 13, 2025.

Share-price momentum remains strong—its ETF Grade in that category is an “A,” according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. KBWB can be a risky product, however, and investors endured intense volatility in the sessions after Liberation Day, but those who held the fund were rewarded in the weeks following the April 7 intraday bottom. Finally, the ETF sports solid liquidity metrics—its average daily volume is high at above 2.2 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is low at just three basis points, per Invesco.

For an update on the portfolio, the 2-star ETF by Morningstar plots on the far-left side of the style box, indicating its high weight to the value style. Forty-four percent of the allocation is considered in the “SMID” cap space, which adds to its cyclical exposure. Still, the big banks—think JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Wells Fargo (WFC), Morgan Stanley (MS), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Bank of America (BAC)--are the real drivers of KBWB price action.

The ETF sports a very low 11.0x price-to-earnings ratio, while its long-term EPS growth rate is robust at 11.6%, resulting in a compelling PEG ratio under 1x.

KBWB: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

I expect KBWB to feature modest volatility through the end of the first half of 2025. Bank earnings are done, and market-wide implied volatility gauges have retreated substantially from their April zeniths. From a longer-term perspective, I’m encouraged to see that the fund’s dividend payout has increased since 2020. That tells me that banks are rewarding shareholders, despite bouts of macro volatility in the last five years.

Overall, the allocation is somewhat concentrated—there's more than 60% invested in the top 10 stocks.

KBWB: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal perspective, May and June tend to produce lukewarm returns when scanning price data over the past 10 years. July is among the best months of the year, so perhaps we see a rally in advance of and through bank earnings season, which begins in mid-July.

KBWB: Muted Q2 Returns Historically

The Technical Take

With positive macro news regarding the Basel III rules and given KBWB’s attractive valuation, the technical situation is mixed, but broadly encouraging. Notice in the chart below that shares held key long-term support during the post-Liberation Day tumble. The low was $51, and the bank ETF trades above $66 today. This whippy price action comes as the long-term 200-day moving average remains trending higher—that tells me the bulls control the primary trend.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph—it's now above 70, helping to confirm the nearly 30% rally off the April nadir. I see resistance at a pair of spots. The first is the $71 to $73 zone, which was the double top from late 2024 and this past February. The second is the all-time high, which dates back to January 2022. There is a downside price gap near $57, so that should act as support if the key $60 mark does not hold.

KBWB: Shares Back Above the 200dma, $72 Resistance, Strong RSI Trends

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on KBWB. The apparent news that gold will be counted as a Tier 1, High-Quality Liquid Asset is a net positive for banks. It allows them to embark on higher-ROI projects while maintaining proper capital ratios. With a low valuation and decent chart, I expect gains over the balance of the year.

data.title: Pinterest: Unsustainable Growth And User Backlash Signal Danger Ahead
data.description:
The Setup

Pinterest (NYSE:PINS) recently sold off from ~$40/share in February to the mid-20s in April because of macro and tariff concerns. The stock recovered some of its losses leading up to earnings, and subsequently jumped to $32/share after Q1 earnings were announced on May 8th. Shares stabilized in the $29 to $30 range on May 9th before rallying to ~$33/share on May 12th after the US and China announced a 90-day tariff reduction.

The initial PINS sell-off seems warranted because advertising dollars are highly macro-sensitive. Also, tariffs are problematic because they impact Pinterest's e-commerce advertisers. E-commerce companies will theoretically advertise less on Pinterest because of tariffs, as selling products sourced from China may no longer be profitable. However, tariff concerns were brushed aside during the latest earnings call when Julia Donnelly, Pinterest’s CFO, said, “There are small pockets of spend that have been impacted by tariffs in recent weeks.”

Although the market liked Pinterest’s recent earnings and the tariff announcement, I am more bearish about the company’s long-term outlook. In my view, the narrative presented by management is overly optimistic.

Pinterest’s Revenue Growth Isn’t Sustainable

Management’s 3–5-year revenue growth targets were set at mid-high teens during Pinterest’s 2023 Investor Day. Sell-side estimates for revenue growth remain in the mid-teens for 2025, 2026, and 2027, according to CapIQ.

However, most revenue growth is driven by Pinterest’s US and Canada (UCAN) average revenue per user (ARPU) growth.

ARPU growth has been primarily driven by ad load growth, an unsustainable source of growth because Pinterest can only show users a finite number of ads. Signs of excessive ad load are already showing. ARPU was $6.54 in Q1’25 for UCAN – 8% YoY growth relative to $6.05 in Q1’24, a substantial decrease from the 13.9% YoY UCAN ARPU growth in 2024 (UCAN ARPU growth is calculated in this Excel file using data from Pinterest's 10-K).

Additionally, Reddit is flooded with complaints about high ad load. To compare ad complaints over time, I used a Google account with no search history and used the query “Pinterest site:reddit.com.” The date range was limited to one-year periods. Then, I looked at the first 10 search results and determined the percentage with complaints about too many ads.

Users are not happy with Pinterest’s direction. Notably, the first 10 search results for other platforms (Facebook, Snapchat, etc.) primarily focused on establishing an online presence on those platforms.

My view of sustainable US revenue growth aligns more closely with US user growth. US ARPU growth slowing down could take 5pps or more off annual revenue growth and cause Pinterest to miss sell-side estimates. My view of US user growth is pessimistic because users are unhappy with the increase in ad load (as shown in the chart above). Users are also dissatisfied with low-quality AI content and worsening search functionality, which I will explain in more detail later.

Ad Pricing is at Risk

Pinterest has significantly increased the volume of ads on its platform. Ad impressions were up 49% YoY, but ad prices were down 22% YoY. Management claimed that most of the negative price impact was due to a mix shift resulting from international growth. When asked about UCAN ad pricing, though, Pinterest’s CFO was vague in her response. Julia Donnelly said, “If you were to look at kind of UCAN impression growth and pricing alone, it would tell a very different story, and the trends are far less pronounced because the primary driver on a global level really is this mix shift impact due to international growth.” So, while UCAN ad prices aren’t down by >20% per year, the vagueness of Julia’s response indicates that prices are flat to down in my opinion.

Going forward, ad pricing is likely to be pressured as Pinterest continues to increase its ad load. After all, there is a finite number of relevant ads that Pinterest can display to users.

Rebuttal to Bullish Arguments

Bullish investors will likely counter by claiming that the Rest of the World's growth will compensate for potentially declining US ARPU growth. However, much of Pinterest’s international revenue growth is driven by ad load growth, so international revenue growth is likely not sustainable at current levels. Additionally, international growth is of relatively low value and potentially margin-dilutive, as Pinterest needs to allocate engineering resources to service other countries while generating very little revenue. GDP per capita is much lower in Pinterest’s Rest of the World countries, so advertising space is less valuable.

The bulls would also argue that Pinterest is utilizing AI to target ads more effectively at users. This argument appears weak because users are dissatisfied with the volume of ads on Pinterest. It doesn’t matter if ads are slightly more relevant when users want a higher ratio of user-generated content, but all they see are ads. They will eventually vote with their feet.

The User Experience Flywheel is Exaggerated

Management argues that ads are additive to the user experience. Therefore, more ads lead to a better user experience, which in turn leads to increased engagement, resulting in more advertisers, and ultimately, more engaging ads.

Pinterest’s CEO argues that ads are additive because Pinterest users have “commercial intent.” However, the data indicates that Pinterest users have little commercial intent. As noted earlier, users are unhappy with the increased ad load. Pinterest’s average cost per click is also significantly lower than that of its competitors, further suggesting a lack of "commercial intent."

Additionally, other parts of the flywheel are questionable. It’s a stretch to say that more advertisers lead to more effective and engaging ads in my opinion. More advertisers may mean that Pinterest can show the user more relevant ads, but this impact is likely minimal.

The User Experience Is Declining, so the “Flywheel” Could be in Reverse

Ads

As mentioned earlier, users are very unhappy with the increased ad load. Even employees dislike the focus on ads.

Low-Quality AI Content

Pinterest’s user base is extremely unhappy with AI content flooding their feeds. To approximate the increase in AI complaints over time, I used the same “Pinterest site:reddit.com” query and looked at the first 10 posts (note: multiple posts complained about ads and AI simultaneously).

Pinterest is aware of the AI problem and has taken steps to combat it. However, it seems that they’ve been largely unsuccessful because their software, which automatically detects AI-generated content, has been prone to false positives and is suspending legitimate accounts.

The AI problem will worsen over time as people become more familiar with AI image tools. AI could pose an existential risk to Pinterest because Pinterest users, who are often seeking inspiration, are strongly opposed to AI-generated content.

Worsening Search Functionality

To approximate the increase in search functionality complaints over time, I used the same “Pinterest site:reddit.com” query and looked at the first 10 posts (note: multiple posts complained about ads, AI, and search functionality simultaneously).

An example of a user complaint is below.

Where could I be wrong?

The primary drivers of Pinterest’s intrinsic value are ARPU and user growth. If management can leverage AI to expand ARPU growth and incorporate features that enhance the user experience, Pinterest could outperform my expectations for ARPU growth and user growth. It’s also possible that management figures out how to deliver sustained ARPU and MAU growth in Pinterest’s international markets, resulting in a larger contribution to overall revenue growth.

Also, Pinterest's average cost per click and cost per thousand impressions are well below its competitors. If management figures out how to close this gap, the stock could potentially be undervalued at current levels. However, history has shown that Pinterest users are far less valuable than users on other platforms.

Valuation

Revenue Model

I break revenue growth into ARPU and user growth for each geography. I have ARPU growth slowing to a more sustainable rate of 5% for Europe and the US, whereas ARPU and user growth stay elevated in the rest of the world. I model relatively stable user growth in the US and Europe. This is likely an optimistic assumption given user experience issues.

EBIT Model

I assume that G&A stays at $488 million (average of 2023 and 2024) to model in operating leverage. Additionally, I assume a slight decrease in R&D as a percent of revenue because international expansion will require more R&D resources, but Pinterest is heavily investing in AI personnel. There's room for investment in AI personnel to moderate over time. I assume S&M and COGS are more variable.

Relative Valuation

Pure-play comparables for Pinterest don't exist. Pinterest is a combination of a social, search, and e-commerce business. Meta and Google are of much higher quality than Pinterest, but Snapchat and Etsy are lower quality, so the average multiples indicate where Pinterest should trade.

Pinterest looks fairly valued using EV/Sales multiples but overvalued using EV/EBIT multiples. Using the comps' average EV/Sales multiple might overvalue Pinterest because Meta pulls the peer group average up. As a result, my view is that $14.73-$30.07 is a fair valuation range for Pinterest (which excludes the Comps Average EV/Sales Multiple scenario).

Conclusion

Given the risks involved, Pinterest looks expensive at 5.3x EV/Sales (TTM). ARPU growth is likely unsustainable and has already weakened. User experience complaints are also everywhere, but have yet to appear in MAU data. Excessive ads and AI content will likely only get worse as time progresses - therefore, I'm bearish on Pinterest and believe it's worth closer to the bottom end of my $14.73-$30.07 valuation range.

data.title: Trinity Industries: A Good Company In A Challenging Environment
data.description:
Investment thesis

The Seeking Alpha summary page for Trinity Industries, Inc. (NYSE:TRN) includes a bright red banner warning: “TRN is at high risk of performing badly”.

I argue in this article that investors should check out the warning, but it should be just one of several factors to consider in making a buy or sell decision. In my examination of the warning, I found it was of limited value.

As for Trinity’s prospects and valuation, both are good, but I am concerned about challenges from tariffs and a potential economic slowdown. I rate it a Hold.

About Trinity

It is a major player among railcar lessors and manufacturers in North America. Founded in 1933, it operates under the tradenames of TrinityRail, RSI Logistics, and Holden America.

It operates through two segments, Railcar Leasing and Services Group, and Rail Products Group. The former provides what it calls in the 10-K for 2024, “comprehensive railcar industry services”. The main business of this group is leasing freight and tank railcars.

The latter, Rail Products Group, manufactures freight and tank railcars for the North American market. It also has a parts and components business and it holds patents applicable to manufacturing railcars and components.

At the close on May 13, shares traded at $26.79 and it had a market cap of $2.16 billion.

Competition and competitive advantages

According to the 10-K it faces “aggressive competition”, on the basis of performance, technological innovation, quality, reliability of delivery, customer service, and more. I note that this list does not include price, although that might be expected in a highly competitive market.

Competitors include The Greenbrier Companies, Inc. (GBX), GATX Corporation (GATX) (which is also a customer for Trinity-manufactured railcars), and Union Tank Car Company, a subsidiary of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B).

Despite the competition, the company said in its 10-K, “We believe we are a leader in the rail industry digital transformation by providing digital and terminal management services designed to increase the efficiency and visibility of the supply chain, while leveraging data, insights, and analytics to make decisions that improve operations and reduce costs.”

To assess its competitiveness, we will compare its net income margin and return on equity with those of Greenbrier and GATX. First, net margins:

The standout in this group is obviously GATX, while Greenbrier and Trinity are more than 10 points behind; as the chart legend shows, Trinity is about a point below Greenbrier.

When it comes to returns on equity or ROE, the positions are reversed, with Greenbrier and Trinity leading GATX:

Based on this information, I would assess Trinity as having a narrow to medium moat.

First quarter results

Trinity released its Q1-2025 earnings report on May 1, 2025. These are the key points:

  • Quarterly revenue of $585 million, down from $809.6 million in Q1-2024.
  • Net income of $24.0 million, down from $28.0 million in 2024.
  • Diluted EPS GAAP of $0.29, down from $0.33 in 2024.

Here’s more detailed information, from the earnings report:

The poorer results mainly stem from a serious dip in Rail Products Group revenue. In Q1-2025 it had revenues of $420.5 million, down from $667.4 million in Q1-2024. On the Q1 earnings call, President and CEO Jean Savage said,

“Our results in this segment reflect the current operating environment. We delivered 3,060 new railcars in the quarter and received orders for 695 railcars, evidence of the delayed investment decisions I have previously acknowledged and the lumpiness of orders quarter-to-quarter. As a result, quarterly revenue was down due to lower deliveries.”

Delayed investment decisions: That’s Trinity’s challenge at the moment, and it could be deep-seated. As Matt Watters and Shubham Singhal noted in an April 23, 2025 article for the consulting firm, McKinsey, “The uncertainty about the tariffs and possible retaliations or negotiations have created one of the greatest levels of peacetime uncertainty for US businesses.”

As a result, I don’t believe delayed investment decisions will reverse in the near future, which means continued challenges for Trinity’s top and bottom lines.

On the other hand, there are several positives about Trinity’s situation. First, fleet utilization is high, at just under 97.0%, with management saying on the earnings call that the North American railcar fleet is in balance. In this state, there is no excess capacity and pricing is rational.

Second, it has been able to push up its lease rates and still enjoy high renewal levels. According to management, “Lease rates are driven by many factors, but the most important is a balanced fleet.”

Third, it had a backlog of $1.9 billion at the end of the first quarter. That’s reassuring for a company that had revenue of $3.079 billion last year.

The dividend

While existing shareholders are no doubt discouraged by the sagging share price, new buyers at least have the consolation of a rising dividend. Indeed, the price has fallen far enough for the dividend yield to reach 4.53% at the close on May 13, when the price was $26.79.

The dollar amount is $1.20 per year and it has grown by an average of 10.01% per year over the past five years. It has increased for 14 consecutive years.

The warning

Returning to the Seeking Alpha system warning about Trinity “performing badly”, it lists two characteristics associated with poor future stock performance. First, declining growth, and second decelerating momentum. What’s more, growth has declined enough to generate a Sell rating from the Quant system.

Let’s review its revenue, net income, and basic EPS over the past five years:

Yes, revenue has declined over the past three quarters, but as the chart illustrates, that’s after 11 consecutive quarters of increases.

Net income has dipped, slightly over the past two quarters, but the second dip is relatively small. EPS fell from $187.4 million in Q3-2024 to $157.1 million in Q4-2024, and to $156.8 million in Q1-2025. So, really, there has been only one problematic quarter in the past nine quarters.

Basic EPS basically reflects what has happened with net income over the past three quarters, but the drop was more severe in Q3-2024. Again, we have one bad quarter in the past nine quarters.

Apparently, then, what the company has done or not done is not likely responsible for the warning. That leaves momentum; has it been a problem? Well, it was, and I stress the past tense:

As this table from the Seeking Alpha system shows, there is a clear distinction between what has happened in the past week and month, and what has happened over longer periods.

It appears the data in this table reflect the market downdraft that occurred as tariffs were being discussed, imposed, retracted, and imposed again. It further suggests that concern about tariffs and a potential recession peaked, and then settled back down again.

Finally, note that Trinity has no direct influence over momentum, it emerges out of the actions of millions of individual investors and thousands of firms including the biggest institutional investors.

How much weight should we give this warning? Investors considering buying or selling the stock should certainly read it and assess it. But, I believe it should be just one part of thorough due diligence.

Trinity’s growth prospects

This slide from the Q1-2025 presentation shows management’s guidance for full-year 2025:

Turning to the specifics, it forecast about 35,000 railcar deliveries in the Q4-2025 results; it has now cut that to 28,000-33,000 deliveries. At the midpoint of 30,500 cars, that’s reduction of about 12.86% one quarter later.

Net fleet investment remains the same at $300-$400 million.

Operating and administrative capital expenditures estimates remain the same from Q4: $45 million - $55 million.

Q1’s forecast of EPS ranging from $1.40 - $1.60 is down from Q4’s forecast of $1.50 - $1.80. Using the two midpoints, $1.50 and $1.65, the company is guiding 10% lower now than it did three months earlier. Presumably, that reflects mainly the dip in railcar deliveries.

From another perspective, Railcar Leasing and Services is relatively immune because most of its leases are long term and thus less affected by short-term uncertainty. However, economic uncertainty is slowing orders for new railcars, and pulling down segment revenues.

Therefore, I think it is a reasonable possibility that Trinity’s earnings could go even lower.

According to a report from TD Cowen and cited in Yahoo!Finance, shipping volumes at U.S. ports are down significantly. The west coast has been hit hardest, with volume down 30%. On the east coast, volume has slipped 12%.

The decline is mostly driven by retail and apparel firms temporarily ending their shipments of non-essential goods from China. Some companies did move freight/inventory in the first quarter, and that will exacerbate the situation in the second quarter and beyond.

The problem for Trinity, of course, is that when there is no shipping from China, there is reduced need for railcars to move containers and other freight within the U.S. And when railcars aren’t moving they are not wearing out or as in demand.

Such thinking may have influenced the normalized EPS estimates of Wall Street analysts, who see 2025’s result near the bottom of the company’s forecast range:

I think there is little doubt that 2025 will be a challenging year for Trinity, but by how much and for long are questions we are unable to answer now.

Valuation

The share price peaked at $39.83 in January 2025, about the time when concern about tariffs began to take hold. As the one-year chart shows, it has had many more down days than up days in recent months:

A quick sidenote: While the 52-week high is listed as $39.83, the Historical Prices (Charting page) shows the high as $39.45. This discrepancy appears to originate with the raw data provided to Seeking Alpha.

With the price down, it earns better valuation grades. Notably, its P/E ratios are below those of the Industrials median:

Given the undervaluation, drooping earnings for this year, and the fact Trinity is a well-managed company, I’m going to assume the price will be about the same in 12 months. That means, of course, some ups and downs, but at the end of 12 months to be near where it is now.

Given this situation, I rate Trinity a Hold. There have been no other Seeking Alpha analyst ratings in the past 90 days. The Quant system has had a Sell rating since April 28; previously it had rated it a Hold.

Even the usually bullish Wall Street analysts have a Hold rating, although they expect the price to rise by 9.91% over the coming year. Along with that go one Down revision and no Up revisions.

Risk factors

The biggest current risk to Trinity is the tariff situation, and the associated uncertainty. With the market for rail shipments already declining because of the tariff war with China, demand for new railcars will continue to lag.

The warning about the risk of performing badly may become reality, although I think the concern is somewhat overblown. As we saw, it mostly pertains to momentum, and that has turned in the past month and week.

In this article, I make several assumptions about the future, as do the company and Wall Street analysts. Those assumptions may prove to be incorrect if there continue to be abrupt changes in the political and economic environment.

Trinity operates in a world of aggressive competition, and failure to make technological progress, to use an appropriate business model, or to let its costs get too high could lead to loss of market share.

In the 10-K, it points to the fact its customers operate in cyclical markets. In turn, that means unpredictable demand and volatility. In particular, if markets for energy products, agricultural products, and consumer goods all slowed at the same time, there could be serious repercussions for the company.

Conclusion

I believe Trinity Industries is a strong, well-managed company that should reward shareholders in coming years. At the moment, though, it faces a couple of challenges. First, the slipping share price has triggered a warning to investors, which I consider overblown. It is also challenged by tariffs and the uncertainty they generate.

While it is a good candidate for growth investors, it may take several quarters or more to see it get back on the growth path. I rate Trinity a Hold.

data.title: Trump's Fiscal Austerity: A Shock To The Economy And Market
data.description:
The US fiscal deficit has been growing at an alarming rate in recent years. It reached -6.28% of GDP (annual) in the fiscal fourth quarter of 2024. This figure has actually grown to -7.0% of GDP (annual) by the end of March 2025 (12-month deficit of $2.1 trillion divided by nominal annualized GDP of $29.977 trillion). This represents the largest peace-time fiscal deficit, while at full employment, in the history of the US.

Virtually all analysts agree that this level of fiscal deficits during a period of full employment is completely unsustainable. The question is: What, if anything, will President Trump do to address this extremely important problem? And if he does anything about it, how will it impact the economy?

In this article I will show that a combination of inertial effects, President Trump’s discretionary executive policies and the upcoming congressionally-approved budget could cause a major fiscal spending shock to the US economy – one that could trigger a US business cycle recession.

An Objective Assessment of the Current Situation

After the US fiscal deficit exploded from -4.6% of GDP in 2019 to -14.7% in response to the COVID pandemic in 2020, successive US Congresses and Presidents failed to reign in the US budget deficit from 2021 through 2024, despite strong economic growth during this period. For example, from 2022 to 2024, US government employment increased by about 1.2 million or an average growth rate of nearly 3% per annum. This was one of the highest 3-year growth rates for government employment since the 1960s. This even more eye-catching given the relatively sluggish rate of private employment in 2022-2024 versus the 1960s.

As a result of the out-of-control government spending from 2022 to 2024, the fiscal deficit as a percent of US GDP is currently at a level of 6.28% -- which is a peace-time record during a period of full employment. Indeed, this rate of budget deficit as a percent of GDP is greater than almost all other peacetime fiscal deficits (as a percent of GDP) during periods of recession and high unemployment.

If anything at all is done to reduce the fiscal deficit as a percent of GDP to a more sustainable level, this will necessarily cause a “drag” on US GDP growth. A fiscal drag on GDP growth is particularly difficult to overcome when the economy is operating at or near full employment and when economic activity is already relatively weak.

In this regard, it is important to note that reducing the US fiscal deficit as a percent of GDP does not require that the fiscal deficit (measured in US dollars) be reduced; it only requires that the growth rate of the fiscal deficit decelerate to a rate that is slower the growth rate of US nominal GDP. For example, if the US fiscal deficit grows by 1.0% and US nominal GDP grows by 4.5%, then the fiscal deficit as a percent of GDP will decline, even though the deficit itself (in dollar terms) actually increased.

With this very important principle in mind, we need to proceed to for forecast the US’s fiscal situation going forward, and the potential impact of fiscal policy on US economic growth. We do this in two parts. First, we forecast the “inertial impact” of fiscal policy on US economic growth, if the status quo were maintained. Second, we adjust this estimate for any changes that President Trump and the Republican Congress may bring about.

Inertial Effects of Status Quo Would Deliver a Major Fiscal Shock

Let us imagine that no changes whatsoever would be made to the laws in place prior to the election of President Trump that impact fiscal revenues and expenses. What would happen to the US fiscal deficit as a percent of GDP, and how would it impact US GDP growth? This is what we will refer to as the “inertial” effects that were in place prior to the election of President Trump and the Republican Congress.

Assuming no changes were made to forecasted expenditures and revenues based on already-enacted legislation, the US budget deficit was expected to grow in 2025. However, it was expected to grow by a rate that was less than the growth rate of nominal GDP. As a result, the inertial effect of the status quo would have been for US fiscal policy to exert a “drag” on US GDP growth in 2025.

In order to estimate the potential impact of “fiscal drag” on the US economy during 2005, we turn to the Hutchins Center, which tracks this effect carefully on a continual basis through their Fiscal Impact Measure [FIM]. This measure of the impact of overall fiscal policy on US GDP growth translates changes in taxes and spending at federal, state, and local levels into changes in aggregate demand, in order to quantify the effect of historical and projected fiscal policy on US real GDP growth.

As can be seen in Figure 1, using projections from the non-partisan Congressional Budget Office (CBO) and assuming no significant changes in legislation already approved by the prior Congress and Presidential Administration, fiscal policy is expected to exert a substantial “fiscal drag” on US GDP growth in 2025.

Figure 1: Fiscal Impact Measure

Indeed, this negative “fiscal drag” was already felt in the first quarter of 2025. Based on the FIM measure, a slowdown in the rate of growth of the US fiscal deficit subtracted 0.63% from US GDP growth in Q1 2025.

Going forward, the inertial fiscal drag is expected to be much more severe. For example, in the second quarter of 2025, fiscal policy is expected to subtract -1.41% from GDP. In Q3 2025, the negative impact of fiscal policy on GDP growth is expected to be -1.16%. In Q4 2025, fiscal drag is expected to be -1.25%.

To be absolutely clear, readers need to understand that it is not expected that the US fiscal deficit will be reduced during the remainder of 2025. It is only expected that the growth of the fiscal deficit will slow if the status quo were preserved. It is this slowdown in growth of the fiscal deficit that would be expected to make a negative contribution to US GDP growth. For the remainder of 2025, this “inertial” drag on GDP is estimated to be on the order of -1.3% of GDP.

This is a pretty significant fiscal shock for the US economy, which only has a potential growth rate of roughly 2.0% during periods of full employment.

Could Trump Policies Add to the Fiscal Shock?

It will not be possible to accurately forecast the impact that President Trump and the Republican Congress will have on the fiscal deficits until tax and spending legislation covering fiscal year [FY] 2026 are passed by the US congress. It is important, in this regard, to understand that until October 2025, US fiscal policy (and its impacts on US GDP) will be mainly determined by budgets that were passed by the Congress in 2024 and which were signed into law by President Biden.

However, it is possible to make some informed conjectures about what could happen to the US budget deficit and its impact on US GDP growth, based on the budget that President Trump has proposed and based on the legislation that is currently working its way through both chambers of the US Congress.

Austerity Shock in Fiscal Year 2025

Between now and the end of the fiscal year (September 30, 2025), fiscal policy is mainly supposed to be determined by legislation that was passed by Congress and signed into law by President Biden in 2024. However, as a matter of fact, fiscal policy during the first calendar quarter of 2025 and for the remainder of FY 2025 (April 1 – September 30) was and will be significantly impacted by various executive actions put into effect by President Trump.

The first major executive actions that have impacted fiscal policy in 2025 are a series of spending cuts carried out as a result of actions and/or recommendations by the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). First, USAID and a few other minor government agencies have been terminated, restructured or substantially defunded. Second, a voluntary retirement program for government employees was implemented, which had a reasonably substantial uptake. Third, various cuts aimed at eliminating waste and fraud have been implemented across numerous departments and agencies based on the recommendations of DOGE.

The impact of DOGE-related spending cuts implemented and/or announced thus far are estimated to be on the order of $100 billion. Further actions resulting in spending cuts are expected during 2025.

The second major executive action impacting fiscal policy in 2025 has been the implementation of tariffs on most US imports. Tariffs are expected to increase government revenues by approximately $200 billion over a 12-month period.

The combination of DOGE-related spending cuts plus tariff-related revenue increases – a roughly $300 billion annualized impact on US fiscal policy in calendar year 2025 – amount to a pretty large austerity package. Considering that the US budget deficit is running at an annual rate of approximately $2 trillion, these measures would amount to a 15% reduction in the budget deficit on an annualized basis.

Austerity Shock in Fiscal Year 2026

It is important to note that any legislation passed by the new Republican Congress and signed into law by President Trump will not go into effect until Fiscal year 2026, which starts on October 1, 2025. What this means is that any tax measures currently being debated in Congress won’t start having much of an impact on the economy until April 2026. However, any spending cuts and/or spending increases approved by Congress could start having an impact in October 2025.

The timing of fiscal impacts is important to consider since it highlights the fact that fiscal austerity (tariffs, executive spending cuts and congressionally-mandated spending cuts) will probably be “front loaded” during calendar year 2025. By contrast, any tax relief that is approved by Congress as part of the “Big Beautiful Bill” that is currently working its way through Congress will only start to stimulate the economy starting in April 2026.

Note that this timing may not be coincidental. The timing of the implementation of fiscal austerity measures may be designed to create the sensation of a booming economy – relative to the 2025 economy -- around the time of mid-term elections in November of 2026.

In this article, I will only focus on the potential fiscal shock in calendar 2025. Thus, in considering the Fiscal Year budget for 2026, we will only consider items that could impact the economy during calendar year 2025.

Let’s start by considering President Trump’s proposed “Skinny Budget” for fiscal year 2026. It targets a freeze in the growth of discretionary spending in FY 2026. It specifically proposes big spending cuts in non-defense discretionary spending at certain government agencies and departments. In total, Trump is proposing $163 billion in cuts to non-defense discretionary spending items – a decrease of -23% from the already-enacted 2025 level. This includes massive cuts to various “woke” initiatives (e.g. climate initiatives, promotion of DEI and LGBTQ+), the Environmental Protection Agency, the Department of Education, the State Department, foreign aid, and the Department of Housing and Urban development, among others.

In principle, a large chunk of these spending cuts could be implemented in the fourth quarter of 2025. And it makes political sense that they would be front-loaded because Republicans would like the economy to start recovering by early 2026 and booming by the time of the November 2026 elections.

Trump’s proposed cuts to non-defense spending will be offset by increases in the budgets for the Department of Defense and the Department of Homeland Security (mainly for purposes of border security and deportation efforts).

Simply leaving overall spending unchanged for the 12 months of FY 2026 would represent a fiscal drag on US GDP during that period of time. Due to the timing of the midterm elections in 2026, I wonder whether the Trump Administration will choose to “sandbag” some of the budget increases to the Defense Department in 2025 and “back-load” them into 2026 to help boost the economy in time for the 2026 mid-term elections?

If so, this would amplify the fiscal shock during 2025.

It is unclear whether and to what extent Trump’s proposal to freeze discretionary spending in FY 2026 will be approved by Congress. However, President Trump has suggested that he will hold the line on non-defense government spending via executive actions even if Congress passes legislation providing for an increase in non-defense discretionary spending. Trump could legally accomplish this if it is done in furtherance of efficiency and prevention of waste and fraud.

There are a few other sources of potential government austerity measures that are currently being considered by Congress that could potentially impact the economy adversely in 2025. First of all, some spending cuts may be achieved via the tightening of certain requirements for receiving Medicare, Medicaid and other Social Security benefits. Some of these restrictions can be put into effect right away. Second, legislation is being considered that would raise taxes on university endowments and non-profit organization. It is likely that if those measures are passed, universities and non-profits will lower their spending in 2025, as their operating budgets for 2026 will be severely impacted, and they will have to save cash in 2025 in order to compensate.

Offsetting these austerity measures are certain tax cut proposals, including the elimination of taxes on overtime and taxes on tips. These two measures could start creating some stimulus to the economy as soon as October 2025.

The precise net impact of all of these measures is, as of yet, unclear. But the pattern is pretty discernible. Fiscal austerity measures will be more or less front-loaded, creating a very significant “fiscal drag” on the economy in 2025.

The Net Fiscal Shock in 2025

Let’s review. First, we have a negative “inertial” fiscal drag of GDP of around 1.3%, simply based on the slowdown in the fiscal deficit that was already written into law -- plus a partial allowance for the impact of tariffs and other measures already announced. Second, we have the combined impacts of front-loaded fiscal austerity measures (executively implemented and congressionally approved) that will impact the rest of 2025 to the tune of around -2.0% of GDP (annualized).

Thus, we are talking about a potential net negative contribution of fiscal policy of around 3.30% of GDP on an annualized basis for the remainder of 2025. And this does not consider any potential “multiplier” effects. Multiplier effects are the “knock-on” effects that could be created by the fiscal austerity measures.

Can The US Economy Avert Recession in 2025?

Fiscal spending is not the only thing that affects the US economy. For example, personal consumption expenditures represent the largest share of the economy. Personal spending slowed considerably in Q1 2025 and gloomy consumer sentiment reflected in surveys such as the University of Michigan’s suggests we should be cautious about expending too much from this particular driver of US GDP.

What about exports? It is pretty clear that US exports should be expected to be a negative contributor to GDP growth, given that the rise in US tariffs can generally be expected to cause other nations to have less wherewithal and/or desire to import US goods and services.

What about imports? It is true that imports may fall due to tariffs, and falling imports boost GDP in an accounting sense. But declining imports are not really a positive for the economy in the short term as many jobs are tied to the transport, marketing and sale of imported goods. Declining imports are a sign of declining aggregate demand. And this decline in aggregate demand will likely be reflected in lower expenditure, profitability and employment for US businesses and workers.

How about investment spending? In recent months, many companies and even sovereign nations have announced increased investments in the US. But all of these announcements are clearly political and non-binding in nature. We are very skeptical that the announced investments will significantly boost total investments in the US for the remainder of 2025, if at all. Indeed, there is good reason to suspect that investment in equipment could actually be quite sluggish for the remainder of 2025, given that much investment expenditure on equipment was front-end loaded in Q1 to avoid tariffs. Furthermore, residential investment may be “dead in the water,” for the rest of 2025 due to high mortgage rates, high inventory and stagnant demand.

In sum, even under the best of circumstances, it would be extremely difficult for the US to be able to avert a recession in the fact of a negative fiscal shock of -3.3%, particularly under conditions of full employment. But the current conditions are far from ideal. Prospects for personal consumption, business investment and exports do not appear to support expectations that they could overcome a fiscal shock of the magnitude expected Even if we simply assume an “average” performance from personal consumption and business investment, the US is likely to experience at least a mild recession in 2025. And if personal consumption and/or business investment were to perform below average or actually contract, the resulting recession could be fairly sharp.

It should be noted that it is particularly difficult for the economy to avoid a recession when there is a negative fiscal shock under conditions of full employment. Fiscal drags contributing to deceleration of economic growth a played a significant role in the run up to the recessions of 1990, 2000 and 2008.

Conclusion

I would like to caution that it is not possible at this juncture to precisely quantify the fiscal shock that will be weighing down the economy in 2025. The extent of the shock will depend on discretionary executive and legislative actions. However, the outlines of a potential shock in 2025 are becoming increasingly clear.

A fiscal drag effect already had a quantifiable impact of -0.63% of GDP on an annualized basis in Q1 2025. This negative impact of fiscal policy contributed significantly to the contraction of US GDP of -0.3% recorded in Q1 2025. Going forward, the fiscal drag on the economy during 2025 will be much more severe.

We expect that the negative contribution from fiscal policy will be on the order of -3.30%, on an annualized basis, for the remainder of 2025. We believe that this shock will be driven by a combination of inertial effects, necessary fiscal austerity measures and politically motivated timing effects.

We do not think that personal consumption, investment or exports will be able to offset (if there is any offset at all) our expected fiscal shock. Therefore, we think at least a mild business cycle recession is likely.

In this context, we think US stocks are in for a nasty surprise, as the combination of exuberant price action and valuations are clearly indicating that investors are not expecting any sort of recessionary scenario in 2025. As a result, starting in a few weeks, we think that indices such as S&P 500 (SPX) and Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) are likely to begin another major decline below recent lows and into bear market territory. In particular, we think that Magnificent 7 stocks such as Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), Nvidia (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA), could see substantial contractions in their valuations as they reflect growth expectations that are unlikely to materialize.

Final Thoughts

At Successful Portfolio Strategy, we are of the view that 2025 will be a year of great turbulence in the equity markets – with major declines, followed by sharp rallies. This is an ideal environment to implement active investment strategies that are able to capitalize on the tremendous volatility while simultaneously lowering drawdown risk. For example, our timely entries and exits on gold mining stocks and our timely entry into short volatility positions via instruments such as -1x Short VIX Futures ETF and (SVIX) and ProShares Short VIX Short-Term Futures ETF (SVXY) have enabled us to achieve substantial positive returns in 2025, despite the fact that we have assumed substantially lower risk overall risk than those that have been fully invested in US stocks during this period. We believe that 2025 will continue to be present many "out-of-the-box" opportunities that will enable investors to massively boost their absolute and relative long-term performance.

data.title: Mayhem Continues
data.description:
A 90-day pause on embargo-style US-China trade tariffs has revived bullish spirits. The large-cap S&P 500 (black below), with its one-third weight in tech companies (“Magnificent Seven” companies in orange), has rebounded 18% since April 8—now flat year to date and -3.8% below the February high.

The retail crowd is back at it, bidding the most popular meme stocks (pink below) up 25% since April 11, while the Goldman Sachs retail favourites basket (in blue) has rebounded 16.96%.

It’s worth remembering that such dramatic rebounds are typical during ongoing bear markets, as the most-shorted stocks, which have fallen the most, get repurchased on profit-taking from short-sellers.

In the real economy, small business hiring plans and job openings have not rebounded (below, from 2017, courtesy of The Daily Shot).

Nor have capital expenditure plans (below since 2017) that touched the COVID-lows in April. Making long-term capital allocation commitments is tough when government policy is whipsawing daily.

It’s hard to get a word edge-wise with David R., but his economic content is always worth a mull. This discussion covers many relevant observations.

Disclosure: No positions.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Google Ups Privacy And Intelligence Ante With Latest Android Updates
data.description:
Beauty, as they say, is in the eye of the beholder. In the latest updated versions of Google's (NASDAQ:GOOG, NASDAQ:GOOGL) Android 16 and Wear 6 operating systems, for many, undoubtedly, the beauty will be found in the new Material 3 Expressive design language. It is, after all, a dramatic and attractive change versus previous user interface designs from Google. Plus, the level of detail on things, like how well the elements of the screen automatically adjust to your interactions in an intuitive and intelligent way, is definitely impressive.

However, the true inner beauty of the new OS stems from the under-the-hood changes that Google has integrated into the Android experience - some of which will be arriving very shortly. In particular, Google has integrated the intelligence of its generative AI-powered Gemini assistant throughout all its latest Android iterations, and it has incorporated an extensive range of security and privacy-related features into Android 16 as well.

To be fair, the Gemini integration had already started with Android 15 on a few select devices, but Android 16 expands that to all Android phones and tablets - without a subscription - and it extends the experience to smart watches running Wear OS 6, the latest version of Android Auto, and even Google smart TVs running Android.

On all these devices, you’ll soon be able to have the kind of voice-based natural language interactions that LLM-powered chatbots have started to get us all accustomed to. In environments like the car or in active situations when you’re wearing a smart watch, that can make a big difference in improving the usability of the overall Android experience. The addition of support for Gemini Live also means you can engage in lengthy conversations on virtually any topic while in those settings. Furthermore, few people who watch TV simultaneously have access to a keyboard, so accurate responses to voice-based queries are important in these situations as well.

The latest version of Gemini in Android 16 is bringing in visual capabilities, meaning Gemini can see and react to what’s on your device’s screen or what it sees through the camera. Most people have just begun to experiment with these kinds of multi-modal interactions, and they’re bound to be blown away by what the latest AI-powered assistant technology can do. Google even briefly hinted at bringing these capabilities to Android XR mixed reality devices, which it’s going to be talking about in more detail at next week’s Google I/O event.

On top of the intelligence features - which stand in stark contrast to where Apple (AAPL) currently finds itself with Siri and Apple Intelligence - Google also made a surprisingly broad range of announcements related to privacy and security for Android 16. From scam detection and personal identity authentication to mobile theft protection and other anti-fraud capabilities, the latest version of Android has an impressive array of new tools designed to keep people and their devices safer.

While they’re certainly not as pretty as a new user interface, these kinds of features offer a compelling set of deterrents that the huge number of people whose lives have become utterly dependent on their devices should find very attractive. The unfortunate truth is that the type, number and sophistication levels of scamming and other privacy-related attacks targeting people’s smartphones has grown extensively over the last few years. As a result, these types of privacy and security capabilities have quickly migrated from a nice-to-have to an absolute essential, and it’s great to see Google stepping up here.

For example, Google can now identify potential fraud in text messages and even during live phone calls to prevent bad actors from being able to surreptitiously take over a device or steal data. These features can also save people from unwittingly changing settings or sharing data that could let their devices or information be compromised. Real-time app scanning can also stop certain threats from being started from sideloading or remote loading of applications. A new Identity Check feature, first available for Google Pixel and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) devices, offers stronger protection even in the event a PIN or password is compromised along with the phone being lost or stolen.

Google also expanded its little-known Advanced Protection capabilities in Android 16, which are designed for the most at-risk or security-conscious individuals, including public figures, journalists, and others who want to be shielded from today’s most sophisticated digital threats. New features including automatic intrusion logging of potential security events for further analysis, disabling auto-reconnect to insecure networks, securing USB ports from physical attacks, and more. All of these are on top of the existing protections in Advanced Protection, which offers an inter-connected group of security settings that can be easily turned on at once.

Finally, Google upgraded its my Find My Device service to what it’s calling Find Hub. In addition to the traditional capabilities for tracking lost devices and smart tags, Find Hub adds the ability to track the location of friends and family (with their consent and with the appropriate privacy-related settings to avoid unrecognized tracking). The company also announced upcoming support for smart tags with Ultra-Wideband (UWB) technology, satellite-based tracking and new types of embedded tags in things like skis, luggage and more.

All told, it’s a comprehensive set of updates that demonstrates Google’s continued moves to step outside the shadow of Apple and iOS, particularly here in the US market. After years of being seen as a laggard in many types of technologies and user interface, as well as thought of as more prone to security-related concerns, the realization of announcements like these will make Android a clearly viable and arguably even more advanced alternative to the Apple hegemony. The new capabilities of Gemini, in particular, bring a level of AI-powered intelligence that can really make the modern Android experience an impressive one. Toss in the ability to consistently use Gemini across a huge range of devices, and the story becomes even more compelling.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Farmland Partners: Outlook Increased As Company Diversifies Revenue Streams
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Farmland Partners Inc. (NYSE:FPI) has underperformed the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), delivering a loss of ~11%:

I previously covered Farmland Partners back in December 2024 with a Hold rating, arguing that despite an increase in AFFO (driven by deleveraging and cost savings), the AFFO multiple remained too high relative to other commercial REITs. Recent underperformance justifies my view, although I would note that the decline appears excessive in light of the surge in the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD), which is up ~24% so far this year. I continue to believe that farmland should offer stronger long-term returns relative to gold thanks to its current cash flows and safe-haven status.

In this article, I share my highlights from the REIT's recently released Q1 2025 results. I confirm my Hold rating for the shares as the company's valuation remains quite high relative to both farmland peers, as well as the broader commercial REIT universe.

Company Overview

You can access all FPI results on its website here. Farmland Partners is a farmland REIT that owns 92,415 acres, primarily in the Corn Belt (46% of the portfolio) and the High Plains (23%). In addition, the company manages 46,751 acres for third parties:

60% of the REIT's land is used to grow primary crops (corn, soybean, wheat, rice, cotton), while 40% is devoted to specialty crops (citrus, avocados, tree nuts).

To increase returns, the company has also recently been increasing its lending to farmers, with loans and financing receivables accounting for 7% of the REIT's total assets.

The REIT's enterprise value totals $770 million, of which 64% is funded with common equity, 13% with preferred shares, and 23% with net debt.

Q1 2025 Results Overview

Farmland Partners reported AFFO of $0.05/share, down 16.7% Y/Y, driven in large part by a decline in net operating income /NOI/ by 16% Y/Y. This was due to lower property operating revenues (down 14.5% Y/Y) as the REIT has been downsizing its portfolio in recent quarters:

Even so, the company increased its full-year 2025 outlook to $0.28-0.34/share. The improved outlook is the result of higher interest income and larger solar, wind, recreation rent. Recent share repurchases also contributed to the higher AFFO per share:

Conference call highlights

The most bullish factor for FPI remains the discount to net asset value (NAV) of the portfolio, with management reiterating their rationale for share repurchases at current levels:

NAV of the portfolio, I haven't directly addressed this in the last couple of conference calls, so a couple people asked me so I'll do it here. We would say that the portfolio today is in the mid-14s, just kind of connecting the dots and to explain a little bit why we quit talking about it.

When we look our – at the gap between the NAV, as Paul indicated, and our stock price, we continue to believe that our own stock is effectively the cheapest farmland we can buy, and we will probably continue to do so as appropriate and as it makes sense.

I believe the discount to net asset value can largely be explained by the need to compensate for administrative overhead. Case in point, in 2025 the REIT is set to spend almost $12 million on general and administrative/legal expenses.

Another point worth mentioning is that the REIT is boosting its lending in a bid to grow AFFO:

What that means is we have shifted some of our investment to making a few more loans as opposed to property purchases, because the loans of course give us quite a bit higher current yield than owning a farm.

Company valuation

Farmland Partners trades at 33.5x its 2025 AFFO midpoint, one of the highest AFFO multiples you can find for a commercial REIT. Of course, this is the result of the company's modest leverage, with 64% of enterprise value funded with common equity.

Turning to enterprise-level cash flows, the company's Q1 2025 report shows that there are 99,000 preferred shares outstanding, carrying an interest rate of 3%. Annual interest due to the preferred shares is $3 million. Together with the $10.3 million in interest expenses and circa $14.6 million in AFFO, we see that cumulative cash flows to enterprise value total roughly $28 million.

Relative to the $770 million enterprise value, we can calculate that the REIT's market-implied cap rate stands at about 3.6%, which is quite low for a farmland REIT. For reference, Gladstone Land (LAND), which I covered recently, offers a market-implied cap rate of about 5%.

All in all, I still believe it will be quite hard for FPI to outperform VNQ over a long period of time. This is principally the result of a low starting market-implied cap rate and a NOI growth outlook close to inflation. As such, I confirm my Hold rating on FPI.

Risks

Recent land sales have reduced Farmland Partners' debt, and the company remains well-positioned to take advantage of the weakness in its share price via share repurchases. That said, I should note that the preferred shares are eligible for conversion starting in February 2026, which may have a dilutive impact on NAV upside. You can think about it this way - currently, the REIT has 47.2 million shares, which are eligible to capture upside of roughly 39% should the REIT reach its NAV of around $14.5/share. This represents a fair market value for the common equity of $685 million.

If the preferred shares are converted to common shares, shares outstanding will increase by roughly 9.6 million, reaching 56.8 million. In this scenario, the fair market value for the common equity would indeed jump to $785 million, but on a per-share basis, NAV will slump to roughly $13.80/share. Hence, the upside relative to the current share price will dip to only 33%. The company may address this risk by redeeming more preferred stock.

Farmland Partners may also be affected by ongoing trade turmoil, given the surplus of agricultural exports the United States enjoys with the rest of the world. This may result in difficulties for farmers to pay rent, as well as credit losses on the REIT's growing loan portfolio.

Conclusion

Farmland Partners reported weaker AFFO in Q1 2025 but boosted its full-year outlook, driven by non-traditional revenue sources such as lending and solar, wind, recreation rent, as well as ongoing share repurchases.

The company's valuation in terms of AFFO multiple has improved following recent land sales but remains well in excess of other REITs held in the VNQ ETF. As such, I continue to believe that over the long term, it will be quite challenging for FPI to meaningfully outperform the VNQ ETF.

Nevertheless, the company remains appealing for investors putting an emphasis on safety and capital preservation. Thus, I confirm a Hold rating for the shares.

Thank you for reading.
